speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandfather, Abraham Strauss. He is my father's father. And this picture was given to me by one of my aunts. I have no pictures of him because of the Holocaust. All of our family pictures were lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea what year this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture probably was taken in 1930, '32. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] These pictures are pictures of my grandfather, Jacob Andhom and Rosalia Andhom. My grandfather, Jacob was born in Gmunden [INAUDIBLE]. And my grandmother was born in [INAUDIBLE] in [INAUDIBLE]. Pictures you see were taken approximately in 1930, '32. And my grandmother died in 1933, and my grandfather in 1934.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're buried in Gmunden in a Jewish cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year those pictures were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in 1932. Pictures were taken in 1932. This is a group picture of-- of men who were, together with my father, in the First World War. The picture was taken approximately in 1916 or '17 in front of a bunker on a Russian front. My father is the man-- the third one from the left on the bottom. He was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what he was in the army. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture is a remnant of a picture which survived of my mama and my father. The picture was taken in about 1934 or 1935, when they were in-- on vacation in Frankfurt. I believe that's the last time they were on vacation. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture shows our living house, our living quarters, which would be the building to the right, and our business building, which is the building to the left. The building to the right, where we lived, was built in 1700 and, I believe, '26. And it's still standing today, is in existence. The building to the left was built in 1909 by my grandfather. And it was our place of business, where we had a dry goods store or a all around type of country store, carrying anything from clothes to food to leather goods. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture depicts me when I was five and a half or six years old. It was taken in 1931. And it was my first day in school. I'm carrying the traditional schultute, which all children carried on their first day to school. The picture was given to me by my aunt who had immigrated to the United States in 1933. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my little sister and myself. It was taken around 1929. In Gmunden behind our house, there was a river. And as you can see, there is a bridge where the water flows through. And it was always a happy place to be and play. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My sister's name is Greta. And of course, I'm Ludwig. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture is a picture of my Aunt Flora, Flora Andhom. It was taken in 1938. It was a kennkarte picture, a picture for an identification card all Jews had to carry. It's the only picture I have. It was taken from the files of the mayor's office after I was liberated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my Aunt Bessie, Bessie Andhom, sister of my mother. The picture was taken in 1938 for the purpose of identification for an identification card. I had no pictures of her, but I retrieved this picture from the files of the German authorities after the war. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of my sister, the only picture I have of her when she was 12 years old. This picture was also taken for identification purposes. I retrieved it from the German files in 1945 after the war. She was 12 years when this picture was taken. Want me to say? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's name is Greta. You-- the picture of-- of-- the man you're seeing is my father, [? Izzie, ?] [? Izzie ?] Strauss. This is also the last picture I have of him. It was taken by the Germans in 1938 for identification purposes. I retrieved it from the official records in Germany in 1945 after the war. The next picture you'll be seeing is the picture of my mother, Bella Andhom, Bella Strauss [? Capone ?] Andhom It is also the last picture I have of my mother. It was also taken in 1938 for identity card purposes. And I also retrieved it from German files after the war in 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The pictures we see here are pictures of myself and my cousins, as well as my mother and aunt. These pictures were taken 1935. Of all of these people on these pictures, three of these people are alive. All the rest of them perished during the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The first on the left of the group picture sitting is myself. That picture was taken in 1935. The second is my cousin Ruth, who in 1936, went to Palestine. The third one is my sister, who did not survive. The fourth is Ruth's sister, Ilsa, who did not survive. The fifth is a cousin of mine, Berthold, who lives in Florida, who did survive. The top right is a picture of my aunt, who did not survive. My mother, on the bottom, obviously did not survive. And the three cousins on the picture of three on the right-hand side are Ilsa, Inga, and Helmut. None of them survived, either. They all perished during the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my wedding picture, or a wedding picture of myself and my wife, Jeanine. This picture was taken at our wedding in New York City on September the 13th, 1958. And I have lots of good memories from that. This picture is of my aunts and uncle. On the left is my Aunt Frieda, Frieda Metzler, who I lived with when I came to the United States. My uncle Ludwig is Ludwig Strauss. And to the right is my Aunt Guste, Guste [? Guggenheim. ?] All three are brothers and sisters of my father. They came to the United States in the early and middle '30s, except my uncle, who, in 1939, went to Cuba and eventually, in 1941, immigrated to the United States. All of them are no longer alive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a very happy picture for me. It was a surprise party my wife made for my 70th birthday. My son came in from Washington, DC, on the left. His name is Kenneth Strauss. He is a doctor. My wife is Jeanine, next to him. And I am next to my wife, Bert. And to the right of the picture is my younger son, Mark, who is living here in Los Angeles with us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And the picture was taken on April the 29th, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is our pride and joy, Jeremiah, our grandson, son of our older son, Kenneth. He is 9 and 1/2 years old now and a lot of pleasure to all of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this picture was taken in 1994.
